questions.question_text: СS A 35-year-old man comes to your office with symptoms of nasal congestion and itchy eyes and throat. He has been experiencing such symptoms for several years. Symptoms are present throughout the year, and he is able to enjoy outdoor activities without worsening of the symptoms. He owns a cat, which does not sleep in the same room with him. You order allergy skin testing and receive a report indicating a positive response to dust mites and cat dander. Which of the following therapeutic interventions is the most effective for this patient's symptoms?

answers.text: Removal of the allergen from the patient's environment

questions.question_text: СS An 18-year-old man comes to clinic complaining of nasal stuffiness, left-sided maxillary tooth pain, and postnasal drip. He has had these symptoms for more than 2 months. After the first 2 weeks of symptoms, he was seen in a walk-in clinic and given a 5-day course of antibiotics, but his symptoms did not improve significantly. He has not had fever or chills but complains that he wakes up with a sore throat on most days; the throat pain tends to get better as the day goes on. On examination, he is afebrile, with mild tenderness to palpation over the left maxilla and left forehead. His posterior oropharynx is slightly erythematous, with yellowish drainage present, but there is no tonsillar exudate. Examination of the nares reveals hyperemic mucosa and mucopurulent discharge. Which of the following statements regarding this patient's condition is true?

answers.text: Chronic sinusitis can be defined as sinus inflammation that persists for more than 3 weeks
Sinus radiographs are the procedure of choice for evaluating patients suspected of having chronic sinusitis
It is likely that anaerobic bacteria are the primary pathogens responsible for this patient's condition
Nasal culture has sufficient sensitivity and specificity to guide further antimicrobial therapy
In patients with medically resistant chronic sinusitis, further workup for conditions such as cystic fibrosis, structural abnormality, or fungal infection is appropriate

When asked, she says that each individual hive lasts for 2 or 3 days. Physical examination reveals multiple urticarial papules that do not blanch on diascopy. You ask the patient to come back to your clinic after 3 days, and you confirm that some of the lesions are still present. On the basis of this patient's history and physical examination, what would be the next step in the workup?

answers.text: Perform an abdominal CT scan to rule out an intra-abdominal malignancy
Check sinus films, hepatitis serology, and stool studies for ova and parasites
Perform a biopsy of one of the lesions
Check autoantibodies specific for systemic diseases

You learn that she has a history of allergy to peanuts and that she may have eaten some without knowing it. On physical examination, the patient is alert and is in moderate respiratory distress. Her blood pressure is 90/50 mm Hg, and her heart rate 120 beats/min. She has diffuse inspiratory and expiratory wheezing, and she is experiencing diffuse urticaria. What is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?

answers.text: Administer oxygen and start I.V. steroids and I.V. fluids; the flight can be continued
Start an I.V., inject 1 mg of epinephrine I.V., and give I.V. steroids, I.V.fluids, and oxygen; the flight can be continued

Administer oxygen and epinephrine subcutaneously or intramuscularly,give I.V. antihistamines and I.V. fluids, start steroids, and ask the pilot to land and transport the patient to an emergency care facility
Give oral antihistamines and oral prednisone and continue to watch the patient for further clinical deterioration
Give oral prednisone and continue to watch the patient for further clinical deterioration

Physical examination is remarkable for the presence of a diastolic and systolic murmur in the mitral area. Transthoracic echocardiography shows a vegetation in the mitral valve. questions.question_text: The following data are used for the diagnosis of vitamin D-deficient rickets:

answers.text: Functional state of the cardiovascular system

questions.question_text: The physiological effects of 1,25(OH)₂D₃ at the muscular level include:

answers.text: Increased ATP availability in muscle tissue

questions.question_text: Which factors reduce the effectiveness of solar ultraviolet radiation?

answers.text: Increased air pollution or dust concentration

questions.question_text: The physiological effects of 1,25(OH)₂D₃ at the level of bones include:

answers.text: Regulation of calcium and phosphorus mobilization in coordination with parathyroid hormone

questions.question_text: The following degrees of severity are distinguished in vitamin D-deficient rickets:
Vitamin D-deficient rickets is characterized by the following changes in long bones:

answers.text: Deformities in the shape of “O” or “X”

questions.question_text: Positive diagnosis of vitamin D-deficient rickets is based on:

answers.text: Normal or decreased serum calcium levels
Decreased levels of vitamin D metabolites (25-hydroxyvitamin D₃, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D₃)

questions.question_text: Which factors influence bone lesions in vitamin D-deficient rickets?

answers.text: Decreased resistance of bone to mechanical loading
Insufficient mineralization of the organic bone matrix
Increased calcium concentration in bone tissue

questions.question_text: Acute evolution of vitamin D-deficient rickets is characterized by:

answers.text: Predominant occurrence during the winter–spring period
Predominance of osteomalacia in bone lesions

questions.question_text: Subacute evolution of vitamin D-deficient rickets is characterized by:

answers.text: Predominance of symptoms related to osteoid tissue hyperplasia

questions.question_text: Features of skeletal system involvement in rickets include:

answers.text: Predominant skull involvement during the first 3 months of life
Predominant thoracic involvement between 2 and 6 months of age
Involvement of long bones after 6 months of age
Deformities of the lower limbs appearing after 3 months of age
O- or X-shaped deformities of the legs commonly observed around 1 year of age
